claims.claim: Adapters invoke `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` on core and must not modify the app's behavior.
claims.rationale: A single `fetch` entry point makes the same core run on every runtime.
claims.excerpt: the same `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` signature works everywhere. The adapter's job is to produce that call — not to modify the app's behavior.

claims.claim: Bundle size discipline is required: `hono/tiny` targets sub-12 kB and Workers has a 1 MB compressed bundle limit.
claims.rationale: Justifies the zero-dependency rule because transitive deps are shipped by bundlers.
claims.excerpt: `hono/tiny` advertises sub-12 kB; that only holds if core brings nothing along. On Workers, where the 1 MB compressed bundle limit is real, a single careless dep can blow the budget

claims.claim: CI mechanically enforces: `package.json` `dependencies` must be empty/absent, and `node:`/runtime imports outside `src/adapter/**` (except the context-storage exception) fail lint.
claims.rationale: Both rules are checkable without reviewer judgment.
claims.excerpt: any non-empty `dependencies` field fails CI; any `node:` import outside `src/adapter/**` (with the `node:async_hooks` exception) fails a grep-based lint.

claims.claim: Core code must be built entirely on Web Standard APIs with no runtime-specific globals or imports.
claims.rationale: Ensures the framework loads and runs unmodified on all nine-plus supported JavaScript runtimes.
claims.excerpt: Core code is built entirely on Web Standard APIs. Permitted primitives: `Request`, `Response`, `Headers`, `URL`, `URLSearchParams`, `fetch`, `ReadableStream` / `WritableStream` / `TransformStream`, `TextEncoder` / `TextDecoder`, `globalThis.crypto` (Web Crypto), `AbortController` / `AbortSignal`, `Blob`, `FormData`. No runtime-specific globals or imports.

claims.claim: In-repo adapters are only for runtimes whose type surface is Web-Standard or shipped by the runtime itself (Cloudflare types are devDependency-only).
claims.rationale: Prevents runtime-specific types leaking into the main package.
claims.excerpt: Adapters inside this repo are for runtimes whose type surface is either Web-Standard or already shipped by the runtime (Cloudflare's `@cloudflare/workers-types` is devDependency-only).

claims.claim: Middleware in `src/middleware/**` should prefer Web Standards; unavoidable runtime-specific imports must be cross-runtime in practice.
claims.rationale: Middleware must remain broadly portable across supported runtimes.
claims.excerpt: Middleware in `src/middleware/**` must prefer Web Standards; where a runtime-specific import is unavoidable it must be cross-runtime in practice

claims.claim: Node.js support ships in a separate `@hono/node-server` package rather than inside `src/adapter/` to avoid pulling Node types into the main `hono` package.
claims.rationale: Prevents affecting type resolution for non-Node users.
claims.excerpt: `@hono/node-server` is a separate package even though Node is a supported runtime, because adding it to `src/adapter/` would pull Node types into the main `hono` package and affect every non-Node user's type resolution.

claims.claim: Per-adapter helpers may legitimately import runtime primitives (e.g., `node:fs/promises`, `node:path`, `node:crypto`) because they only run on runtimes that provide those modules.
claims.rationale: Adapter code is scoped to a single runtime, so runtime-specific imports are expected there.
claims.excerpt: Per-adapter helpers legitimately import runtime primitives. `src/adapter/bun/serve-static.ts` imports `node:fs/promises`; `src/adapter/deno/serve-static.ts` imports `node:path`; `src/adapter/lambda-edge/handler.ts` imports `node:crypto`.

claims.claim: Runtime-specific integration must live in `src/adapter/<runtime>/**`, translating native shapes to/from Web Standard Request/Response.
claims.rationale: Keeps core runtime-agnostic while isolating per-runtime translation in adapters.
claims.excerpt: Runtime-specific integration lives in `src/adapter/<runtime>/**`. Each adapter translates the runtime's native event/request shape into a Web Standard `Request`, invokes `app.fetch(request, env, ctx)` on core, and shapes the returned `Response` back into whatever the runtime expects.

claims.claim: The `hono` package must have zero runtime dependencies; new entries in the `dependencies` field are rejected.
claims.rationale: Protects bundle size, supply chain trust, and portability across runtimes.
claims.excerpt: The `hono` package has zero runtime dependencies. `package.json` lists only `devDependencies`. New dependencies in the `dependencies` field are rejected.

claims.claim: The only permitted `node:` import in middleware is `node:async_hooks` in context-storage; future `node:` imports in middleware require explicit ADR-level justification.
claims.rationale: Keeps the exception narrow and auditable; preserves cross-runtime middleware guarantees.
claims.excerpt: The exception is not open-ended — future `node:` imports in `src/middleware/**` require explicit ADR-level justification.

claims.claim: Third-party middleware requiring dependencies must ship outside the core repo under `@hono/*` scope or user-owned packages.
claims.rationale: Keeps dependencies opt-in and prevents affecting users who don't install them.
claims.excerpt: Third-party middleware lives outside this repo. Middleware that needs deps (Zod validation, OpenAPI generation, Sentry, Prisma integration) ships under `@hono/*` scope or user-owned packages.

claims.claim: Utility functionality (cookie/query parsing, MIME detection, base64, JWT, UUID, body reading) is hand-implemented in `src/utils/**` rather than delegated to dependencies.
claims.rationale: Deliberate cost of the zero-dependency rule.
claims.excerpt: Utilities are hand-implemented. Functionality that other frameworks delegate to libraries — cookie parsing, query parsing, MIME detection, base64, JWT signing/verification, UUID, body reading — lives in `src/utils/**`.

claims.claim: Consumers of router output must handle both representations of the dual Result<T> shape
claims.rationale: Implementations may pick either representation, so consumers must support both
claims.excerpt: consumers of router output must handle both

claims.claim: Router output consumers like compose(), context construction, and app.routes introspection must handle both Result<T> shapes
claims.rationale: Assuming one form will break when the active router uses the other
claims.excerpt: Anything consuming router output (`compose()`, context construction, `app.routes` introspection) must handle both representations.

claims.claim: Users may bypass presets by passing any Router<T> directly to the Hono constructor
claims.rationale: The Router<T> interface is a public escape hatch for custom implementations
claims.excerpt: Advanced users may bypass presets by passing any `Router<T>` directly: `new Hono({ router: new RegExpRouter() })`

claims.claim: The dual Result<T> shape is a deliberate optimization to avoid eagerly constructing Params objects per request
claims.rationale: Allows lazy parameter lookup via indices and a stash
claims.excerpt: `ParamIndexMap` + `ParamStash` avoids eagerly constructing per-request `Params` objects for every matched route

claims.claim: All router implementations must implement the full Router<T> interface (name, add, match)
claims.rationale: A shared contract is required for presets, user-supplied routers, and SmartRouter to interoperate
claims.excerpt: All router implementations implement `Router<T>` defined in `src/router.ts`

claims.claim: Benchmarks must cover each router; removing routers or presets without benchmark evidence is not supported
claims.rationale: Comparative data is needed to keep presets' rationale valid
claims.excerpt: Removing routers or presets without benchmark evidence is not a supported maintenance path.

claims.claim: New router implementations must support the full path grammar accepted by any existing router
claims.rationale: Otherwise preset swaps would silently change which routes work
claims.excerpt: New routers must support the full path grammar, including every path shape that at least one existing router already accepts.

claims.claim: Partial router implementations (throwing on certain methods or unsupported path subsets) are not allowed
claims.rationale: Partial implementations would break preset-swap semantics
claims.excerpt: Partial implementations (e.g., a router that throws on certain HTTP methods, or doesn't support a subset of path patterns the other routers accept) are not allowed.

claims.claim: Preset entry points are a public API commitment; removing a preset is a breaking change
claims.rationale: Downstream users import from these paths
claims.excerpt: Removing `hono/quick` or `hono/tiny` is a breaking change for every downstream user importing from those paths.

claims.claim: Presets are separate module graphs, not runtime configuration flags, so bundlers ship only selected router code
claims.rationale: Ensures deployments don't pay bundle cost for unused router code
claims.excerpt: they're different module graphs so the bundler ships different code

claims.claim: RegExpRouter throws UnsupportedPathError on path shapes it cannot encode
claims.rationale: Enables SmartRouter to detect incompatibility and fall back
claims.excerpt: throws `UnsupportedPathError` on path shapes it can't encode

claims.claim: Runtime behavior may differ between presets; documentation must flag universally-supported path shapes
claims.rationale: PatternRouter relies on platform URLPattern with different semantics than RegExpRouter+TrieRouter
claims.excerpt: Documentation must flag which path shapes are universally supported across all presets.

claims.claim: SmartRouter mutates its own name on first match; introspection code must tolerate this transition
claims.rationale: router.name changes from 'SmartRouter' to e.g. 'SmartRouter + RegExpRouter' after selection
claims.excerpt: Tests, dev tools, and introspection code that read `router.name` must tolerate this transition.

claims.claim: SmartRouter selects a router on first match, locks to the winner, and rebinds its match method to bypass dispatch afterward
claims.rationale: Trades a one-time selection cost to avoid a hard upfront choice
claims.excerpt: On first `match()`, it tries each router in order, catches `UnsupportedPathError`, locks to the first router that succeeds, and hot-swaps its own `match` method to the winner's bound method

claims.claim: The Router<T> interface is public API; breaking changes to it are breaking changes to user code
claims.rationale: Users implement Router<T> themselves for custom routers
claims.excerpt: The interface is their API, not just an internal abstraction. Breaking changes to the interface are breaking changes to user code.

claims.claim: Three preset entry points ship with distinct router strategies: hono (SmartRouter[RegExp,Trie]), hono/quick (SmartRouter[Linear,Trie]), hono/tiny (PatternRouter)
claims.rationale: Presets target long-running servers, edge/cold-start workers, and minimum-bundle deploys respectively
claims.excerpt: Three presets ship as separate entry points

claims.claim: Users needing fully deterministic routing behavior should pass a concrete router instead of a SmartRouter-based preset
claims.rationale: SmartRouter's self-mutation introduces non-determinism in introspection
claims.excerpt: Users needing fully deterministic behavior should pass a concrete router instead of a SmartRouter-based preset.

claims.claim: Calling next() twice within a middleware throws 'next() called multiple times'
claims.rationale: Prevents the class of bugs where middleware calls next in both happy and error paths
claims.excerpt: calling `next()` twice triggers `'next() called multiple times'`

claims.claim: Dispatch detects matchResult[0].length === 1 and invokes the single handler directly with an inline next
claims.rationale: Describes the fast-path dispatch mechanism
claims.excerpt:
The dispatch code in
`src/hono-base.ts:424-442` detects `matchResult[0].length === 1` and invokes
the single handler directly with an inline `next`

claims.claim: Errors thrown anywhere in the chain are caught by compose() and delegated to the app's onError handler
claims.rationale: Defines lexical error propagation semantics
claims.excerpt: Errors thrown anywhere in the chain are caught by `compose()` and delegated to the app's `onError` handler

claims.claim: Hono uses Koa-style onion composition for middleware via compose()
claims.rationale: Chosen model dictates wrapping semantics, error propagation, and composition primitives
claims.excerpt: Hono uses **Koa-style onion composition**. The implementation is `src/compose.ts`

claims.claim: If a handler returns nothing and c.finalized is false at the tail, the onNotFound handler runs
claims.rationale: Defines default not-found behavior at end of chain
claims.excerpt: If a handler returns nothing and `c.finalized` is false at the tail of the chain, the `onNotFound` handler runs

claims.claim: Middleware must call await next() at most once
claims.rationale: Enforced at runtime via compose.ts; double-next is a bug
claims.excerpt: Middleware MUST call `await next()` at most once

claims.claim: Middleware should use await on next(); forgetting await lets downstream handlers run concurrently, violating the onion model
claims.rationale: Neither type system nor compose() catches missing await; linters help but are not foolproof
claims.excerpt:
Forgetting `await` on `next()` is a real class of bugs that neither
  the type system nor `compose()` catches

claims.claim: Middleware signature is (c: Context, next: Next) => Promise<Response | void>
claims.rationale: Defines the uniform contract all middleware must implement
claims.excerpt:
type MiddlewareHandler<E, P, I> = (
  c: Context<E, P, I>,
  next: Next
) => Promise<Response | void>

claims.claim: Most middleware in src/middleware/** does work both before and after next(), making onion composition the dominant pattern
claims.rationale: Justifies choosing Koa-style over Express-style model
claims.excerpt:
nearly every middleware in `src/middleware/**` does work before AND after
  `next()`

claims.claim: Routes that matched exactly one handler must NOT flow through compose(); they use a single-handler fast path
claims.rationale: Saves closure allocation, outer promise, and microtask per request on edge deploys
claims.excerpt:
routes that matched exactly one handler do
  NOT flow through `compose()`

claims.claim: Tests for middleware composition must have at least two handlers in the route; single-handler routes do not exercise compose()
claims.rationale: Two dispatch paths exist; single-handler tests don't cover compose()
claims.excerpt:
Tests that exercise middleware composition
  MUST have at least two handlers in the route; otherwise they don't
  exercise `compose()` at all

claims.claim: The Koa model precludes Fastify-style encapsulation; plugins relying on scope-local lifecycle must be rebuilt as middleware composition
claims.rationale: Accepted tradeoff: Hono's model is simpler and composes across runtimes without a plugin manager
claims.excerpt: The Koa model locks Hono out of Fastify-style encapsulation

claims.claim: The inline comment '// Do not compose if it has only one handler' documents the fast-path invariant
claims.rationale: Codifies the design intent at the dispatch site
claims.excerpt:
documented by the inline comment in
  `src/hono-base.ts:423`: `// Do not compose if it has only one handler`

claims.claim: The single-handler fast-path is an invariant and must not be removed as a perceived duplication of compose logic
claims.rationale: Fast-path is not equivalent to compose() with one middleware because compose() allocates closures and promises unconditionally
claims.excerpt:
The fast-path is an invariant, not an optimization.
  Removing it "because it duplicates compose logic" is a subtle regression

claims.claim: compose.ts is high blast radius; changes should include micro-benchmarks in review
claims.rationale: Every 2+ handler route on every deployment flows through this file; performance regressions affect the entire ecosystem
claims.excerpt:
Reviews of `compose.ts` changes should
  include micro-benchmarks.

claims.claim: compose.ts is kept small (74 lines) so dispatch bugs can be diagnosed by reading the whole file
claims.rationale: Auditability of a high-traffic code path is itself a feature
claims.excerpt: Small file = auditable.** `compose.ts` is 74 lines

claims.claim: onError is per-compose-invocation, not global; mounted sub-apps with non-default errorHandler are wrapped so their errors are caught by their own handler
claims.rationale: Mounted apps maintain their error-handling identity across composition boundaries
claims.excerpt: `onError` is per-compose-invocation, not global

claims.claim: Path patterns must propagate into handler Context via MergePath, including through nested apps mounted with .basePath() and .route().
claims.rationale: So that c.req.param('<name>') is typed from the pattern string.
claims.excerpt: `MergePath<BasePath, P>` carries the route pattern into `Context<E, P, I>`, so `c.req.param('<name>')` is typed from the pattern string.

claims.claim: Adding a route-level feature between `.get(path, ...)` and the handler must extend HandlerInterface overloads for every arity, not just the single-handler overload.
claims.rationale: Partial overload additions leave multi-handler variants broken, which is a regression of the end-to-end inference rule.
claims.excerpt: Adding it to only the single-handler overload leaves the multi-handler variants broken and is a regression per the rule above.

claims.claim: Backwards-incompatible changes to the validator() type signature / how it produces Input belong in major version bumps with migration guidance.
claims.rationale: Third-party validators like @hono/zod-validator depend on this signature; breakage cascades through the ecosystem.
claims.excerpt: Backwards-incompatible changes to how `validator()` produces `Input` break every adapter validator in the ecosystem. These changes belong in major version bumps

claims.claim: Changes to MiddlewareHandler generics at src/types.ts:83+ must preserve Input propagation.
claims.rationale: Local-looking changes (generic defaults, parameter order) can silently break validator→handler inference for every user.
claims.excerpt: Middleware authors touching `MiddlewareHandler` generics at `src/types.ts:83+` must preserve `Input` propagation.

claims.claim: Code generation, runtime schema introspection, and explicit route type annotations were considered and rejected as alternatives.
claims.rationale: Each introduces drift, runtime dependencies, or verbosity that conflicts with the no-codegen, types-as-contract commitment.
claims.excerpt: Alternatives considered: Code generation ... Runtime type introspection ... Explicit route type annotations

claims.claim: Each route registration must return a new HonoBase with `S & ToSchema<...>` so chained routes accumulate a composite Schema losslessly.
claims.rationale: The generic `S` is what `hc<typeof app>` eventually reads; losing accumulation degrades the client surface.
claims.excerpt: so that `app.get(...).post(...).route(...)` chains accumulate a composite `Schema` losslessly

claims.claim: Internal helper bodies may use `any` where necessary for types to compose (e.g., `this as any` in src/hono-base.ts:139).
claims.rationale: Type composition sometimes requires internal escapes that do not affect the declared surface.
claims.excerpt: Internal helper bodies may use `any` where necessary for the types to compose (e.g., `this as any` returns in `src/hono-base.ts:139`)

claims.claim: PR reviews for type changes should include a check that the validator example in the README still infers correctly.
claims.rationale: Provides a concrete sanity check for the validator→handler inference chain.
claims.excerpt: PR reviews for type changes should include a "does the validator example in the README still infer?" check.

claims.claim: Response-shape changes on the server are accepted as compile-time breaking changes for every hc client consumer.
claims.rationale: Typed clients mean the types are the API; breaking changes surface at client compilation rather than runtime.
claims.excerpt: `hc<typeof app>` makes response-shape changes compile-time breaking changes for every `hc` consumer

claims.claim: Route methods (.get, .post, .put, .delete, .options, .patch, .all, .on) are typed via HandlerInterface with overloads for each handler-chain length.
claims.rationale: Each arity threads the merged Input through so validator output flows to the downstream handler.
claims.excerpt: Each route method (`.get`, `.post`, `.put`, `.delete`, `.options`, `.patch`, `.all`, `.on`) is typed via `HandlerInterface` in `src/types.ts:127+`, with overloads for each handler-chain length

claims.claim: The Proxy `get` trap must return `undefined` for the `then` property to prevent the Proxy from being auto-awaited as a thenable.
claims.rationale: Without this, awaiting the Proxy collapses it into a resolved promise; this runtime invariant isn't captured by types.
claims.excerpt: The `get` trap returns `undefined` for the `then` property (`src/client/client.ts:18`) specifically to prevent the Proxy from being auto-awaited as a thenable

claims.claim: The client runtime must remain a minimal recursive Proxy (plus request utilities); no build step, schema registry, or runtime schema validation on the client.
claims.rationale: Part of the product promise: zero build tools and minimal runtime footprint; types come entirely from TypeScript at compile time.
claims.excerpt: There is no build step. The only coupling between server and client is the TypeScript `typeof app` import at type level.

claims.claim: The type-inference chain from route registration through handler Context to typed client must be preserved end-to-end across all four segments (route Schema accumulation, path propagation, validator Input flow, client inference).
claims.rationale: Any break silently collapses downstream types to `any` with no compiler error, destroying the framework's marketed type-safety differentiator.
claims.excerpt: The type-inference chain from route registration through handler Context to typed client MUST be preserved end-to-end.

claims.claim: The typed RPC client must be derived from the server's TypeScript type (`typeof app`) without any code generation step.
claims.rationale: No codegen is a hard architectural commitment; the types are the contract with no fallback escape valve.
claims.excerpt: No codegen is a hard architectural commitment, not a performance optimization.

claims.claim: Type-only tests (expectTypeOf, Expect<Equal<A,B>> assertions) in src/hono.test.ts, src/types.test.ts, and other .test.ts files must not be removed.
claims.rationale: They are load-bearing regression guards for the inference chain; removing them is equivalent to removing runtime regression tests.
claims.excerpt: Removing them to "clean up" the test suite is the same class of mistake as removing runtime regression tests.

claims.claim: Validator output Input (with in/out fields) must merge into downstream handler Context so c.req.valid(target) returns the validated type.
claims.rationale: This inference is what makes validator composition type-safe without codegen.
claims.excerpt: the validator's `Input` merges into the downstream handler's `Context<E, P, I>` so `c.req.valid(target)` returns the validated type

claims.claim: `any` at the declared API type surface (route method return types, Context generics, HandlerInterface overloads, hc return types, validator output types) is never tolerated.
claims.rationale: Internal helper bodies may use `any` for composition, but surface `any` collapses user-facing inference.
claims.excerpt: `any` at the API surface is never tolerated.

claims.claim: `tsc --noEmit` must remain part of the release gate.
claims.rationale: It is the primary protection for the inference chain; removing it removes protection against silent type regressions.
claims.excerpt: Removing the type check from the gate removes the primary protection for the inference chain.

claims.claim: src/types.ts is ~1400 lines and changes there carry wide blast radius; new handler-chain arities require consistent overload additions across every route method.
claims.rationale: Inconsistent overload sets cause subtle inference collapses for specific call shapes.
claims.excerpt: `src/types.ts` is ~1400 lines and changes there carry wide blast radius.

claims.claim: jsx-runtime.ts and jsx-dev-runtime.ts are public API surface; exported names (jsx, jsxs, jsxDEV, Fragment) must not be changed
claims.rationale: TypeScript's JSX transform emits imports from these files automatically; renaming exports breaks every compiled file downstream
claims.excerpt: Changes to their exported names (`jsx`, `jsxs`, `jsxDEV`, `Fragment`) break every compiled file in every downstream app.

claims.claim: Adding a new rendering backend requires a new entry point following the same pattern, not a flag on an existing runtime
claims.rationale: The architecture is explicitly extensible via additional backends, not backend flags
claims.excerpt: requires a third entry point following the same pattern, not a runtime option on an existing runtime

claims.claim: Backend selection occurs at build time via TypeScript's jsxImportSource configuration, not at runtime
claims.rationale: Tree-shaking is insufficient because server and client runtimes have different module shapes with different side effects; entry point must decide
claims.excerpt: **Backend selection is at build time, not runtime.**

claims.claim: Build configurations must route jsxImportSource per-target, often requiring per-package tsconfigs in monorepos
claims.rationale: Each target (server vs client) requires the correct JSX runtime selection
claims.excerpt: Build configs must route `jsxImportSource` per-target; monorepo setups often need per-package tsconfigs.

claims.claim: Bundle size on Workers (1 MB limit) is a real constraint motivating the split-backend architecture
claims.rationale: Shipping a reconciler the server never uses would exceed Workers bundle limits
claims.excerpt: bundle-size problem on Workers, where the 1 MB limit is real

claims.claim: Changes to the JSX subsystem must be verified against both renderers; either-side-only changes are suspect
claims.rationale: The JSX subsystem spans 30+ files and changes frequently need cross-renderer consistency
claims.excerpt: Changes must be verified against both renderers; either-side-only changes are suspect.

claims.claim: Element/Fragment representation, context semantics, common types, and Children utilities are shared across both backends
claims.rationale: Shared element model keeps components portable between server and client
claims.excerpt: Element and Fragment representation (`src/jsx/base.ts`) — both backends consume the same element shape.

claims.claim: Hono ships two separate JSX runtimes behind distinct entry points rather than a single isomorphic runtime
claims.rationale: Server and client have fundamentally different rendering needs; a unified runtime would force server to ship reconciler code or constrain client to string-first abstractions
claims.excerpt: Hono ships **two JSX runtimes behind distinct entry points**, sharing a common element/component model

claims.claim: Hook implementations are duplicated between server (src/jsx/hooks/) and client (src/jsx/dom/hooks/); unifying hooks with legitimately different semantics is dangerous
claims.rationale: Attempts to unify hooks with divergent semantics produce subtle bugs in specific render paths
claims.excerpt: attempts to unify hook implementations that have legitimately different semantics will produce subtle bugs that show up only in specific render paths

claims.claim: Isomorphic apps doing both SSR and hydration must restrict shared components to the intersection of both runtimes' APIs
claims.rationale: Features unique to one side cannot be used by shared components
claims.excerpt: features unique to one side cannot be used by shared components

claims.claim: React-compatibility is a feature of hono/jsx/dom only; hono/jsx does not commit to matching React server semantics
claims.rationale: The server runtime only commits to producing valid HTML streams with Hono's own feature set
claims.excerpt: **React-compatibility is a feature of `hono/jsx/dom`, not `hono/jsx`.**

claims.claim: Shared primitives must have cross-renderer regression tests covering both renderers
claims.rationale: Divergent changes to shared primitives silently break isomorphic components
claims.excerpt: Tests MUST cover both renderers for any shared primitive.

claims.claim: Stateful client hooks like useState and useEffect are not meaningful in the server runtime and are either no-ops or unavailable
claims.rationale: Server renders once per request with no commit phase, making stateful client hooks meaningless there
claims.excerpt: Stateful client hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`) are not meaningful here and are either no-ops or unavailable.

claims.claim: Users must not import DOM-only hooks (e.g., useEffect from hono/jsx/dom) in code that also runs server-side
claims.rationale: Doing so pulls DOM hook code into the server bundle and frequently fails at runtime
claims.excerpt: Importing `useEffect` from `hono/jsx/dom` in code that also runs server-side pulls DOM hook code into the server bundle and frequently fails at runtime.

claims.claim: hono/jsx is the server-side runtime for string-streaming SSR, Suspense streaming, async components, and ErrorBoundary
claims.rationale: Defines the role and scope of the server-side JSX backend
claims.excerpt:
**`hono/jsx` — server-side** (`src/jsx/`)
- String-streaming SSR; `<Suspense>` streaming with boundary placeholders; async components; `ErrorBoundary`.

claims.claim: hono/jsx, hono/jsx/dom, and hono/jsx/dom/client are public package entry points; removing or restructuring them is a breaking change
claims.rationale: Downstream users depend on these as their stable build-configuration contract
claims.excerpt: Removing or restructuring them is a breaking change for every downstream user.

claims.claim: hono/jsx/dom provides the full client-side hook suite and reconciler/scheduler with React-compatibility shims
claims.rationale: Client-side rendering needs hook state, effects, transitions, refs, hydration, and a React-compatible surface
claims.excerpt: DOM mounting, hydration, reconciler, scheduler, full hook suite

claims.claim: hono/jsx/dom/client is a dedicated mount entry point
claims.rationale: Documents the existence and purpose of this separate entry
claims.excerpt: A dedicated mount entry ships as `hono/jsx/dom/client`.
